body.p:
XSLT is an event-driven,ConceptsPush vs. Pull rule-based environment for converting XML data. The advantage of the rule-based approach in particular is not apparent to many developers, and source code is produced that looks like PHP code enriched with XPath.
Apache FOP renders XSL-FO to PDF in two passes.
So why use XSLT at all if you do not use template-match rules, or use them only sparingly?
To clarify this matter, a bit of theory is necessary:

fig.title: Pull Stylesheet
fig.desc: In the "Pull" approach, elements in the source instance are selected and inserted at an appropriate position in the target instance. This approach is comparable to that of template engines such as JSP or ASP. This can be done in multiple stages until the source instance has been gradually converted into the final target instance.

fig.title: Push Stylesheet
fig.desc: In the "Push" approach, the source data is converted step by step into the target data. This approach can be exploratory, and during transformation into an intermediate step, insights are gained that are useful for further processing. Remember: XSLT stands for eXtensible Stylesheet Transformation.

body.p: What has been said so far illustrates the "Pull" approach, but what exactly is being "pushed" is probably still unclear. Let us consider XML in its tree representation.

fig.title: Transformation of the source tree into the target tree
fig.desc: The XSLT processor performs a depth-first traversal and checks at each node it enters whether a rule exists in its rule base that "matches" this node. There are three fundamental ways in which the nodes of the source tree can be copied - or not copied - into the target tree.

body.p: Depth-First TraversalPull stylesheets are commonly programmed with xsl:for-each loops. In my view, this approach is appropriate when no major DTD changes are expected, the XML tree is flat-structured, and the conversion requirements are relatively simple, e.g. when evaluating/converting configuration files. In all other cases, push stylesheets are preferable, i.e. as few xsl:for-each loops as possible and as many template-match rules as possible.
